planting.note: Set out after the last frost from purchased plants.

directions: This vegetatively propagated hybrid is grown from transplants rather than seed, so buy young plants and set them out after frost. It produces tall stems of deep-red flower clusters and keeps blooming through summer heat and into fall. Feed lightly, water when the top inch dries, and cut spent stems back to encourage another wave of bloom.

planting.note: Plant out in early spring; cold hardy once established.

directions: Sow indoors roughly eight weeks before planting and grow the seedlings cool to build sturdy stems. This tall cut-flower type carries rounded purple flower clusters on strong stems ideal for bouquets. Harvest stems just as the clusters open, or deadhead in the border to draw out the blooming season.

planting.note: Set out around the last frost in full sun.

directions: Start seed indoors six weeks before planting, covering it only slightly. This award-winning dwarf shows off white flowers with a striking fire-red center on compact, uniform mounds. Grow in full sun with sharp drainage and remove faded flowers to keep the contrast coming.

planting.note: Transplant near the last frost.

directions: Sow indoors about seven weeks before planting and keep the flat warm until germination. This older award-winning hybrid produces bright scarlet-red flowers on well-branched plants that hold color in warm weather. Give it full sun and steady moisture, and deadhead to keep new buds forming.

planting.note: Set out around the last frost date.

directions: Start seed indoors six weeks ahead, barely covering it and keeping it warm. This compact series bears fringed red flowers on neat plants well suited to pots and front-of-border plantings. Water at the base, provide full sun, and deadhead regularly for continued bloom.

planting.note: Plant out in early spring; flowers the first year.

directions: Sow indoors about eight weeks before the last frost and grow the seedlings cool and bright. This first-year-flowering Sweet William sends up strong stems topped with deep cherry-red clusters that hold up beautifully as cut flowers. Cut or deadhead often, and give plants full sun and good air movement to keep foliage healthy.

planting.note: Transplant in early spring; blooms the first season.

directions: Start seed indoors roughly eight weeks before setting out and keep the seedlings on the cool side. This early-flowering Sweet William produces dense crimson clusters on compact stems and reliably blooms the first year from seed. Deadhead spent heads and grow in fertile, well-drained soil for the best flowering.

planting.note: Set out near the last frost in full sun.

directions: Sow indoors about six weeks before planting, covering the seed lightly. This award-winning selection carries fringed bicolored flowers in shades of purple and white on tidy, weather-tolerant plants. Give it full sun and good drainage, and pinch off old blooms to keep the fresh ones coming.

planting.note: Transplant around the last frost.

directions: Start seed indoors six to eight weeks before setting out and cover only lightly. This striking heirloom pink is grown for very dark maroon flowers edged and marked with white, an unusually dramatic color for the genus. Grow in full sun with sharp drainage and deadhead to keep the dark blooms coming through cool weather.

planting.note: Set out around the last frost date.

directions: Sow indoors six weeks before planting and press the seed lightly into moist mix. This award-winning China pink is unusual for its clustered, semi-double flowers in mixed reds and pinks held above compact foliage. Provide full sun and good drainage, and remove faded clusters to extend the bloom.

planting.note: Transplant around the last frost for a long season.

directions: Start seed indoors seven to eight weeks before planting and keep the mix warm and moist. This free-flowering strain covers airy, well-branched plants with bright pink single flowers and holds up well in summer heat. Shear back lightly after the first heavy flush and deadhead to keep it blooming into fall.

planting.note: Set out near the last frost in full sun.

directions: Sow indoors and cover the seed lightly, holding the flat around 68 degrees until it sprouts. This color-changing selection opens white and deepens toward rose as the flowers age, so a single plant shows several shades at once. Grow in full sun with good drainage and deadhead to keep the shifting color display going.

planting.note: Transplant around the last frost.

directions: Start seed indoors seven weeks before planting and keep it lightly covered and warm. This Japanese-bred strain produces uniform, compact plants with clear red flowers and good tolerance of both heat and humidity. Water at soil level, deadhead regularly, and grow in full sun for the strongest bloom.

planting.note: Start indoors two months before the last frost or sow outside once frost danger has passed.

directions: Barely cover the fine seed and keep the surface lightly moist until sprouts appear in about two to three weeks. Plant out in poor, gritty, fast-draining soil in the hottest, sunniest spot you have and water sparingly. The daisy-like blooms open only in bright sun, so expect a glittering carpet of color from early summer onward.

planting.note: Sow under cover in early spring and transplant after frosts, or scatter directly once soil warms.

directions: Press the tiny seed onto a gritty seed mix without burying it and hold at room temperature until germination. Move seedlings to a sunbaked bed or edge of a gravel path where the roots stay dry. This mix blooms in a jumble of pink, orange, crimson, and white; the petals flare wide on sunny days and stay furled in cloud or evening.

planting.note: Raise transplants indoors or direct sow after the last frost in a dry, sunny site.

directions: Surface-sow onto free-draining compost and keep just moist until the seedlings show. Set plants out in lean, sharply drained soil with full exposure to sun and go easy on the watering can. The bicolored, glistening flowers close at dusk and reopen each bright morning through the warm months.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter and plant out after frost, or sow in place in warm soil.

directions: Scatter the fine seed on the surface of a gritty mix and leave uncovered in good light to germinate. Grow in dry, poor, well-drained ground and full sun for the best show. This selection carries clear yellow petals set off by a dark red central ring, opening only when the sun is out.

planting.note: Set rooted plants out only after all frost has passed and the soil has warmed.

directions: Grow this variegated trailer from rooted cuttings rather than seed, since it will not come true from seed. Plant in containers, hanging baskets, or the front of a sunny bed with light, quick-draining soil and let it dry between waterings. Cream-edged green leaves spill over the pot's rim and small red flowers dot the trailing stems all summer.

planting.note: Sow under glass in spring or direct sow once frost danger passes in a hot, dry spot.

directions: Press the small seed into the surface of gritty compost and keep barely moist and warm to germinate. This species has narrow, grass-like succulent leaves and prefers the driest, sunniest, sharpest-draining soil you can offer. Rose-pink flowers with pale centers open across the low mat whenever the sun shines.

planting.note: Start indoors six weeks ahead or sow outside once the soil is warm and frost-free.

directions: Sow the fine seed on the surface of a sandy mix and keep lightly moist in warmth until sprouts appear. This sprawling succulent is prized for fleshy leaves covered in glistening, water-filled bladder cells that sparkle like frost. Grow lean and dry in full sun; harvest the tangy young leaves for salads, or leave it to trail as a groundcover.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm, dry soil after frost has passed.

directions: Scatter seed thinly over sandy, quick-draining ground and rake in only lightly. This low, succulent annual tolerates salt and drought and forms a spreading mat of small, cylindrical, papillose leaves. Tiny pale flowers open at midday in full sun; keep the soil on the dry side throughout.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings after frost, or grow year-round in frost-free gardens.

directions: Root short stem cuttings in gritty mix, where they take quickly, then plant into baskets or a sunny bank. The trailing succulent stems carry small heart-shaped leaves and shower them with bright magenta-purple flowers that open in sun. Water sparingly and grow as an annual or houseplant where winters are cold.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings out after frost; overwinter indoors in cold regions.

directions: Propagate from cuttings to keep the cream-and-green leaf variegation, which seed will not reproduce. Grow in light, fast-draining soil in full sun and let it cascade from pots or over walls. The variegated foliage is the main draw, accented by small purple-pink flowers in warm, bright weather.

planting.note: Sow outdoors after the last frost once the soil has warmed.

directions: Scatter seed and barely cover it with a light dusting of soil, keeping the surface moist until seedlings emerge. Thin plants to a hand's width apart and give them lean, well-drained ground for the sturdiest stems. Wiry stalks carry little brush-like scarlet tufts from midsummer to frost; deadhead or let it self-sow for a second flush.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost, or start indoors a few weeks early for earlier bloom.

directions: Press seed onto warm soil and cover only lightly, since a shallow start speeds emergence. This selection blooms in a bright true red on slender stems that make it a favorite for cutting; harvest stems just as the tufts open for the longest vase life. Removing spent flowers keeps the plant tidy and blooming well into autumn.

planting.note: Start indoors about a month ahead or sow outdoors once frost danger has passed.

directions: Sow shallowly in warm cells and transplant seedlings out after the weather settles. This form carries soft salmon-to-orange tassels on airy stems, adding fine texture to borders and bouquets. Pinch young plants once to encourage branching, then cut or deadhead often to prolong the display through summer.

planting.note: Sow in warm soil after frost; it thrives in heat and tolerates poor ground.

directions: Surface-sow onto warm, loose soil and keep lightly moist until the small seedlings appear. Also called cupid's shaving brush, it opens dainty lavender-pink tufts and shrugs off heat and lean conditions. It self-seeds freely once established, so pull spent plants if you want to limit its spread.

planting.note: Direct sow in warm soil; it establishes fast in mild, frost-free areas.

directions: Barely cover the fine seed and keep it damp during the week or two it takes to sprout. This warm-climate tasselflower bears small rosy-pink to reddish brushes above a low leafy rosette and blooms quickly from seed. It reseeds readily in warm gardens, so deadhead if you prefer to keep it contained.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring for deep orange-scarlet single flowers by summer.

directions: Press seed lightly into loosened soil and cover only faintly, then water gently to settle it in. This selection carries rich orange-scarlet petals with a satiny sheen on ferny blue-green foliage. Deadhead spent flowers to prolong the display, which begins roughly two months after sowing.

planting.note: Sow in place in spring; blooms in a soft rosy-lilac not found in the wild species.

directions: Sow thinly on the surface and barely cover, keeping the bed damp until germination. Thin plants and let them bake in full sun with sparse watering for the best flowering. The unusual pinkish-purple cups appear about two months from seed and are lovely in wildflower mixes.

planting.note: Direct sow for semi-double, fluted rose-pink blooms edged in deeper color.

directions: Scatter seed over prepared soil and press in without burying deeply. Grow in full sun and lean, well-drained ground, watering only sparingly once established. The ruffled, semi-double pink flowers with picotee edges open about eleven weeks after sowing.

planting.note: Sow in place in spring for creamy white to pale yellow single flowers.

directions: Sow directly on raked soil and cover with the barest sprinkle of earth. Keep the surface moist until seedlings emerge, then thin and let the soil dry between waterings. The soft ivory-cream cups glow at dusk and begin about two months from sowing.

planting.note: Direct sow for warm orange-red single blooms over blue-green foliage.

directions: Press seed lightly into loosened, well-drained soil and barely cover. Grow hard in full sun with minimal water for compact, floriferous plants. The glowing red-orange cups start about two months from sowing and close each evening.

planting.note: Sow in place for rich carmine-rose petals with paler reverses.

directions: Sow thinly where plants will bloom and cover only lightly. Thin seedlings and keep them lean and sunny with sparing water. The deep carmine cups, silvery on the outside, open on bright days beginning about eleven weeks from seed.

planting.note: Direct sow for large, deep orange single flowers on tidy plants.

directions: Barely cover surface-sown seed and mist to keep the bed damp until sprouting. Grow in full sun and poor to average soil, watering little. The broad, brilliant orange cups begin flowering about two months after sowing and self-seed for repeat displays.

planting.note: Sow in place for golden-yellow blooms marked with a deeper orange center.

directions: Scatter seed on firmed soil and press in gently without deep burial. Keep moist until germination, then grow lean and sunny with sparse water. The clear yellow cups with an orange throat resemble wild forms and bloom about two months from seed.

planting.note: Direct sow for semi-double, ruffled apricot flowers washed with pink.

directions: Sow thinly on the soil surface and barely cover, keeping damp until seedlings show. Grow in full sun and free-draining ground with modest water. The fluted, soft-apricot semi-double blooms open about eleven weeks after sowing.

planting.note: Sow in place for compact plants with fluted, semi-double soft-pink flowers.

directions: Barely cover surface-sown seed and keep evenly moist until it sprouts. This Thai Silk selection stays low and bushy with wavy, apple-blossom-pink petals of a silky texture. Blooms begin about eleven weeks from sowing on well-drained, sunny sites.

common_name: Thai Silk Rose Bush California Poppy

planting.note: Direct sow for dwarf plants bearing fluted rose-pink semi-double blooms.

directions: Sow seed thinly on raked soil and cover only faintly, misting to keep it damp. The compact plants carry crinkled, satiny rose-pink petals held above ferny foliage. Flowering starts roughly eleven weeks from seed in full sun with little water.

common_name: Thai Silk Fire Bush California Poppy

planting.note: Sow in place for glowing scarlet-and-gold fluted semi-double flowers.

directions: Press seed lightly into the surface and keep moist until seedlings appear. This dwarf Thai Silk form bears wavy, silky petals in flame tones of red-orange over gold. Bright cups open about eleven weeks from sowing and close on cloudy days.

planting.note: Direct sow for a blend of fluted, semi-double flowers in mixed warm colors.

directions: Scatter the mix on prepared soil and barely cover, keeping damp until germination. Grow in full sun and lean ground with sparing water for the strongest bloom. The ruffled, semi-double flowers range across yellow, orange, pink and cream from about eight weeks on.

planting.note: Sow in place for a mix of single and semi-double flowers in bright and pastel shades.

directions: Sow directly on raked soil and cover with only a whisper of earth. Thin seedlings and grow lean and sunny with little supplemental water. This classic blend offers orange, scarlet, pink, cream and gold cups, some semi-double, beginning about two months from seed.

planting.note: Direct sow for compact plants in a candy-bright mix of colors.

directions: Barely cover surface-sown seed and keep the bed moist until sprouts appear. These dwarf plants suit containers and edging, blooming in a jumble of bright pastels. Flowering begins about eleven weeks from seed in full sun with restrained watering.

planting.note: Sow in place for semi-double, ruffled blooms in soft peach and cream.

directions: Sow thinly on the surface and press in lightly, keeping damp until germination. Grow in full sun and free-draining soil with little water for tidy plants. The frilly, semi-double flowers blend peach, apricot and cream from about eleven weeks on.

planting.note: Direct sow for soft creamy-yellow single flowers over blue-green foliage.

directions: Barely cover surface-sown seed and mist to hold moisture until sprouting. Grow lean and sunny with minimal water for the best flowering. The gentle buttermilk-yellow cups open on bright days beginning about two months from seed.

planting.note: Sow in place for burnished copper-orange flowers with a metallic sheen.

directions: Scatter seed on firmed soil and cover only faintly, keeping moist until germination. Grow in full sun and poor soil with sparing water for rich color. The glowing copper-bronze cups, paler underneath, begin about two months after sowing.

planting.note: Direct sow for intense scarlet-red single flowers on compact plants.

directions: Press seed lightly into raked soil and barely cover, keeping damp until sprouts show. This selection is prized for vivid, pure red cups over finely cut blue-green leaves. Blooms begin about two months from seed and are striking in mass plantings.

planting.note: Sow in place for large, ruffled blooms in blush-pink and creamy champagne tones.

directions: Sow thinly on the soil surface and cover only lightly, keeping moist until germination. Grow in full sun and well-drained ground with modest water. The big, wavy-edged flowers mingle soft pink and pale cream beginning about eleven weeks from seed.

planting.note: Direct sow for pure ivory-white single flowers over ferny foliage.

directions: Barely cover surface-sown seed and keep evenly damp until seedlings emerge. Grow lean and sunny with little water for sturdy, floriferous plants. The clean white cups contrast beautifully with the silvery foliage and start about two months from seed.

planting.note: Sow in place for a dwarf, tufted species with small clear-yellow cups.

directions: Scatter the fine seed on raked soil and barely cover, misting to keep it damp. This compact annual forms low tufts of lacy foliage topped with bright yellow flowers. Blooms open about two months from seed and reseed readily in dry, sunny spots.

planting.note: Direct sow this delicate annual species for small yellow spring flowers.

directions: Sow the seed on the surface and press in lightly, keeping moist until germination. This slender wildflower bears small, bright yellow cups on thin stems. It blooms about seven to ten weeks from seed and is charming in rock gardens and meadow mixes.

planting.note: Sow in place for a low, spreading coastal form with deep yellow flowers.

directions: Barely cover surface-sown seed and keep the bed damp until sprouts appear. This maritime variety stays low and mounding with waxy blue-green foliage that tolerates wind and salt. Deep yellow cups open about two months from seed and it perennializes in mild coastal climates.

planting.note: Sow directly outdoors once the soil has warmed and frost danger has passed.

directions: Scatter seed about an eighth of an inch deep in well-drained soil and keep evenly moist until sprouts appear. Thin young plants to stand roughly a foot apart and give them full sun with little supplemental water once established. The showy white-margined upper leaves color up in late summer above the flowers; wear gloves when cutting stems because the milky sap can irritate skin.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last spring frost when soil is warm.

directions: Press seed lightly into warm, well-drained soil and water gently until germination. This selection is prized for crisp, uniformly white leaf margins on upright stems, so thin plants to a foot apart for the best show. Expect the frosted bract color to peak from midsummer into fall; handle cut stems with gloves to avoid the caustic sap.

planting.note: Sow outdoors after frost, or start indoors a few weeks early for containers.

directions: Sow shallowly in warm soil and keep lightly moist until seedlings emerge. This dwarf, more compact strain suits the front of a border or a large pot, so space plants a bit closer than the tall species. The green-and-white variegated foliage brightens by late summer; snip stems with gloves and sear the ends if using in bouquets.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; blooms earlier than the standard species.

directions: Plant seed just under the surface of warm, freely draining soil and thin to about a foot apart. Selected for quicker color, this strain frosts its upper leaves white noticeably earlier in the season than typical plants. Give it full sun and lean, dry conditions; wear gloves whenever you cut or handle the sap-filled stems.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; a taller cutting-garden selection.

directions: Sow seed a fraction of an inch deep in warm soil and keep moist until it sprouts, then thin to stand about a foot apart. This vigorous cut-flower type throws tall, sturdy stems topped with broad white-edged bracts. Cut stems for the vase once the top leaves have colored, searing the ends to stop the sticky sap; always wear gloves.

planting.note: Sow directly once the soil has warmed and frost danger has passed.

directions: Scatter seed on the surface of loose, well-drained soil and press it in without covering, since light aids germination. Water lightly until seedlings appear, then let the plants dry between waterings once established. The daisy-like red-and-yellow blooms open through summer; snip spent heads to keep new flowers coming or leave a few to self-sow.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost, or start indoors about six weeks earlier for earlier bloom.

directions: Press seed lightly into warm soil without burying it, as it germinates best with light. This compact selection carries fully double brick-red pompom blooms held on tidy, wiry stems. Deadhead regularly and it will flower nonstop from early summer until frost; it thrives in heat and poor, sandy ground.

planting.note: Sow outdoors after the last frost, or indoors six weeks ahead for a head start.

directions: Surface-sow the seed on warm, well-drained soil and firm it gently, leaving it uncovered for light. Domed, double golden-yellow flowers cover this dwarf, mound-forming plant all season. Keep it on the dry side, cut off faded blooms, and expect continuous color from midsummer through the first hard frost.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost passes, or start indoors for transplanting once warm.

directions: Sow on the soil surface and press in lightly rather than burying, since the seed needs light to sprout. This blend produces plants with fully double pompom flowers in a mix of red and gold on compact mounds. Give it full sun and lean soil, remove spent heads, and it will bloom heavily from summer into autumn.

planting.note: Sow directly after frost, or start indoors about six weeks before the last frost.

directions: Barely cover the seed or leave it on the surface pressed into warm soil, as light helps it germinate. This old-fashioned double form bears globe-shaped, frilled flowers in blended tones of red, orange and yellow on taller stems ideal for cutting. Water sparingly once established and deadhead often for a long summer-to-frost display.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost or start indoors six weeks early for quicker bloom.

directions: Press seed into the surface of warm, gritty soil and leave it uncovered for the light it needs to sprout. This dwarf selection forms neat mounds topped with single, red-centered petals rimmed in bright yellow. It tolerates heat, drought and poor soil; shear lightly and deadhead to carry the show from early summer until frost.

planting.note: Sow outdoors once soil is warm, or indoors about six weeks before last frost.

directions: Surface-sow onto warm soil and firm the seed in gently without covering it. This compact, mounding plant carries rich solid-red daisy flowers over gray-green foliage. Grow it in full sun and lean, fast-draining soil, water only when dry, and remove faded blooms to keep flowers forming through the heat of summer.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter or move plants out once nights stay above freezing.

directions: Sow seed barely covered and keep the flat warm and lightly moist until sprouts appear in one to two weeks. Set plants in fast-draining, lean soil in the hottest, sunniest spot you have and water only when the surface dries. Daisy-like blooms open on sunny days from early summer until frost, closing at night and in dull weather.

planting.note: Raise from seed under cover in spring and plant out after the last frost.

directions: Press seed onto a gritty mix and cover lightly, keeping it warm until germination. This stemless South African species forms low rosettes and tolerates drought once established, so err on the dry side. Vivid orange to bronze daisies with dark central rings appear through the warm months.

planting.note: Start indoors in spring; this species is among the more cold-tolerant gazanias.

directions: Sow shallowly in warm compost and prick out seedlings once they are large enough to handle. Give it sharp drainage and full sun, where it may overwinter in milder gardens. Golden-yellow daisies with darker centers bloom over narrow, strappy foliage in summer.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings or plugs after frost as a spreading groundcover.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings at their original depth and let the silvery, low stems knit together to cover slopes and dry banks. Once rooted in it needs little water and thrives in heat and poor soil. Yellow daisies stud the gray foliage through the warm season.

planting.note: Start early indoors for uniform, quick-flowering plants.

directions: Sow seed lightly covered at warm room temperature and grow on in bright light after germination. This compact, early Kiss-series selection holds large solid-orange blooms on tidy plants ideal for pots and edging. Deadhead spent flowers to keep new buds coming through summer.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter and transplant after the last frost.

directions: Barely cover seed and keep warm until seedlings emerge within about two weeks. This dwarf, floriferous selection carries clear yellow daisies on short stems suited to containers and bedding. Give it full sun and lean, well-drained soil and remove faded heads to extend bloom.

planting.note: Raise under cover and set out once the soil has warmed.

directions: Sow in warm, moist compost with only a dusting of covering and grow seedlings in strong light. Rose-pink rays with a darker eye open on this compact, weather-tolerant plant from early summer onward. Water sparingly and site in full sun for the best flower count.

planting.note: Begin seed indoors and move plants outside after frost danger passes.

directions: Cover seed only lightly and hold at warm temperatures for even sprouting. This tidy Kiss selection opens creamy-white daisies with a golden center on short, sturdy plants. Plant in hot, dry, sunny beds and deadhead to keep it blooming into autumn.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter for early color.

directions: Press seed onto warm compost, dust with vermiculite, and keep evenly moist until germination. Bronze-and-yellow striped rays radiate from a dark center on this compact, uniform plant. Full sun and sharp drainage bring out the strongest color and heaviest flowering.

planting.note: Start indoors; this series carries notably large blooms.

directions: Sow seed shallowly in warm conditions and grow seedlings hard in bright light. This large-flowered selection shows white rays with a rosy central flame around a golden eye. Give it heat, sun, and dry feet, and deadhead to keep the big daisies coming.

planting.note: Raise under cover and plant out after the last frost.

directions: Barely cover seed and maintain warmth until seedlings appear, then grow on in full light. Oversized yellow daisies streaked with a red central flame stand out in beds and large pots. Site in full sun with lean, well-drained soil and water only when dry.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter; the Daybreak series opens early in the day.

directions: Sow lightly covered in warm compost and grow seedlings in strong light after sprouting. This selection opens yellow rays boldly striped in red and stays open longer through the day than older strains. Plant in hot, sunny, well-drained ground and deadhead to prolong bloom.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant once frost has passed.

directions: Barely cover seed and keep it warm and moist until germination in one to two weeks. Solid, bright-orange daisies open early and hold well on this uniform bedding plant. Give full sun and dry, gritty soil for the strongest performance.

planting.note: Sow indoors for a striped, multicolored mix.

directions: Sow seed shallowly in warm conditions and grow on in bright light. This mix blooms in a range of colors, each ray boldly striped for a tiger-like pattern. Plant in full sun with sharp drainage and pinch off spent heads to keep flowers forming.

planting.note: Begin seed under cover in late winter.

directions: Cover seed lightly and hold at warm temperatures until seedlings emerge. Soft rose-pink daisies with a paler center open early in the day on compact plants. Full sun and lean, dry soil give the best flowering right through summer.

planting.note: Start indoors; the Talent series is prized for silvery foliage.

directions: Sow seed barely covered in warm compost and grow seedlings in full light. The silver-gray, felted leaves stay handsome even when the yellow daisies are closed. Plant in hot, dry, sunny sites and the foliage-and-flower combination shines all season.

planting.note: Raise under cover and plant out after frost.

directions: Barely cover seed and keep warm until germination, then grow hard in bright light. Rich orange daisies sit above the distinctive silvery-gray leaves of the Talent series. Give it full sun and free-draining soil and avoid overwatering.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter for silver-leaved plants.

directions: Sow shallowly in warm, moist compost and prick out once seedlings can be handled. Warm rose daisies contrast with the woolly silver foliage that defines this series. Plant in a sunny, dry bed and deadhead to keep the display going.

planting.note: Start seed under cover and set out after the last frost.

directions: Press seed onto warm compost with only a light covering and keep evenly moist. Creamy-white daisies with golden centers pair beautifully with the silver Talent foliage. Full sun, heat, and sharp drainage keep both leaves and flowers at their best.

planting.note: Start indoors; the New Day series is among the earliest to flower.

directions: Sow seed lightly covered in warm compost and grow seedlings in bright light. This vigorous, early series flowers quickly from sowing with solid clear-orange daisies. Give full sun and dry, well-drained soil and remove faded heads to keep bloom heavy.

planting.note: Sow indoors early for fast, uniform flowering.

directions: Barely cover seed and keep warm until seedlings appear within about two weeks. Rose-striped rays over a pale ground make a lively pattern on these early, tidy plants. Plant in full sun with lean soil and water only when the ground dries out.

planting.note: Start indoors for an early striped mixture.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and hold warm until germination, then grow on in strong light. This mix blooms early in many colors, each daisy boldly striped down the rays. Full sun and sharp drainage bring the best color and the longest season of bloom.

planting.note: Begin seed under cover in late winter.

directions: Cover seed lightly and keep warm and moist for even sprouting. This early selection blooms in warm bronze and copper tones over compact plants. Give it heat, full sun, and dry, gritty soil and deadhead to extend the show.

planting.note: Start indoors; this strain adds silver foliage to the Kiss habit.

directions: Barely cover seed in warm compost and grow seedlings in bright, airy conditions. Bright daisies rise over silver-frosted leaves that keep the plants attractive all day. Plant in full sun with sharp drainage and deadhead to keep new buds forming.

planting.note: Sow indoors early; the Gazoo series is compact and quick.

directions: Sow seed lightly covered and keep warm until germination within about two weeks. This compact, well-branched selection carries solid clear-orange daisies on short plants. Give it full sun and dry, well-drained soil and pinch spent blooms to prolong flowering.

planting.note: Start indoors early; double-flowered types take a little longer to bloom.

directions: Sow seed shallowly in warm compost and grow seedlings on in strong light. This double-flowered selection produces full, ruffled bronze-and-orange blooms that stay open well. Plant in full sun with lean, sharply drained soil and deadhead to keep the doubles coming.

planting.note: Start indoors for an early, compact bedding mixture.

directions: Barely cover seed and keep warm until seedlings emerge, then grow in bright light. This early, uniform mix flowers in a broad range of colors on short, tidy plants. Full sun and free-draining soil keep it blooming through the summer heat.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter for a striped mixture.

directions: Sow seed shallowly in warm compost and grow seedlings on in full light. Blooms come in many colors, each ray marked with a bold contrasting stripe. Plant in a hot, sunny, dry site and deadhead regularly to keep flowers forming.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings after frost; this named form comes true only from cuttings.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings at their original depth in gritty, free-draining soil in full sun. This striking form has cream rays boldly striped in wine-purple around a dark eye. Deadhead spent flowers and overwinter cuttings under glass in frost-prone gardens.

planting.note: Propagate from cuttings and plant out after the last frost.

directions: Set rooted cuttings into sharply drained soil in the sunniest position available. This distinctive selection carries deep pink daisies with a green-tinged central ring. Keep it on the dry side, deadhead often, and take cuttings in late summer to carry it over winter.

planting.note: Plant out cutting-raised plants once frost danger has passed.

directions: Set rooted cuttings at their original depth in lean, gritty soil in full sun. This soft cultivar opens creamy-white daisies with a golden center over silvery foliage. Water sparingly, remove faded heads, and overwinter cuttings frost-free.

planting.note: Raise from cuttings and set out after the last frost.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in fast-draining soil in a hot, sunny spot. This form shows pale rays with a bold dark central pattern that reads as a contrasting ring. Keep it dry, deadhead regularly, and take late-summer cuttings to overwinter under cover.

planting.note: Propagate by cuttings; plant out after frost.

directions: Set rooted cuttings into gritty, well-drained soil in full sun. This selection produces warm orange daisies over neat, low foliage. Water only when dry, deadhead to prolong the display, and keep cuttings frost-free through winter.

planting.note: Set out cutting-grown plants after the last frost.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings at their original depth in sharply drained, sunny soil. Soft cream-yellow daisies open over gray-green foliage on this tidy perennial form. Keep it lean and dry, deadhead often, and overwinter cuttings where frosts are hard.

planting.note: Propagate from cuttings and plant out after frost.

directions: Set rooted cuttings into free-draining soil in the sunniest position you have. This cheerful form carries bright yellow daisies over low, spreading foliage. Give it heat and dry feet, deadhead spent blooms, and keep stock cuttings frost-free.

planting.note: Raise from cuttings; plant out once frost has passed.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in lean, gritty soil in full sun. This vivid selection opens orange rays each marked with a dark central stripe for a striking pinwheel effect. Keep it dry, deadhead regularly, and overwinter cuttings under glass in colder gardens.

planting.note: Set out cutting-raised plants after the last frost.

directions: Set rooted cuttings into sharply drained soil in a hot, sunny spot. This form bears large golden-orange daisies over compact, spreading foliage. Water sparingly once established, deadhead to prolong flowering, and keep cuttings frost-free over winter.

planting.note: Propagate by cuttings and plant out after frost.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings at their original depth in gritty, sunny soil. This selection produces clear yellow daisies over neat gray-green leaves. Keep it lean and dry, deadhead often, and overwinter stock cuttings where winters are cold.

planting.note: Set out cutting-grown plants once frost danger has passed.

directions: Set rooted cuttings into free-draining soil in full sun and keep them on the dry side. This form carries rich orange-bronze daisies over low, spreading foliage. Deadhead spent flowers through the season and overwinter cuttings under cover in frost-prone areas.

planting.note: Sow indoors in early spring and transplant after all danger of frost has passed.

directions: Press seed lightly into warm soil and keep it evenly moist until germination. This heat-loving species tolerates poor dry ground once established and rewards full sun with warm orange-red bracts. Cut stems for fresh or dried bouquets when the heads are fully colored but still firm.

planting.note: Begin indoors several weeks early for a fuller plant, moving outdoors after frost.

directions: Start seed warm and thin or space seedlings generously since these plants grow tall and bushy. Grow in full sun with sharp drainage and little fuss, and they will bloom nonstop through heat and drought. The airy sprays of small round heads are excellent for cutting and dry beautifully.

planting.note: Sow directly into warm soil after frost, or start indoors for an earlier start.

directions: Scatter seed over warm ground and press it in lightly, keeping the surface just moist until seedlings emerge. This low, spreading plant makes a heat-proof groundcover or edging that shrugs off drought. Small lavender-pink heads appear all summer and self-sow readily in mild regions.

planting.note: Start indoors in early spring and transplant after the last frost for compact edging plants.

directions: Sow shallowly and keep warm until the seedlings appear within two weeks. This dwarf selection stays neat and needs no staking, making it ideal for containers and front-of-border planting in full sun. Deep magenta-purple heads bloom continuously through summer with minimal water.

planting.note: Raise transplants indoors and plant out once nights stay warm.

directions: Barely cover the seed and give bottom warmth for even germination. The compact habit holds up well in mass plantings and pots without flopping. Clean white clover-heads pair with any color and hold their shape when dried.

planting.note: Sow indoors early and move outdoors after the frost date.

directions: Keep seed warm and lightly moist until sprouting, then grow on in bright light. This tidy dwarf needs no pinching and thrives in heat and lean soil. Rose-pink heads bloom from early summer to frost and attract butterflies.

planting.note: Start seed indoors and set plants out after frost for an edging or pot filler.

directions: Sow shallowly in warmth and expect seedlings in one to two weeks. One of the shortest globe amaranths, it forms a low mound perfect for borders and bedding. Bright purple heads cover the plant all season with little care.

planting.note: Grow transplants indoors and plant out into warm soil.

directions: Give seed warmth and darkness until it germinates, then grow in full sun. This very compact form stays under a foot and needs no support. Snowy white heads make a crisp low edging and dry well for crafts.

planting.note: Sow indoors early and transplant once frost has passed.

directions: Press seed lightly into warm mix and keep it dark until sprouted. The dwarf mounded habit suits pots, edging, and tight bedding schemes. Soft pink heads bloom nonstop through summer heat.

planting.note: Start indoors for early transplants and plant out after frost for cutting.

directions: Sow warm and keep dark until germination, then grow on with strong light to build sturdy stems. Bred for the cut-flower trade, this tall uniform strain produces long straight stems on vigorous plants. Harvest heads when fully formed for fresh vases or hang them upside down to dry.

planting.note: Raise indoors early and transplant after the last frost.

directions: Cover seed lightly and provide steady warmth for reliable sprouting. This tall cutting strain yields abundant long stems on heat-tolerant plants. Deep carmine-red heads cut and dry exceptionally well.

planting.note: Sow indoors in spring and set out into warm soil.

directions: Start seed warm and dark, then grow with plenty of light for tall straight stems. This uniform cut-flower selection blooms heavily all summer in full sun. Rose-pink heads hold their color for weeks fresh or dried.

planting.note: Begin indoors early and transplant after frost for cut stems.

directions: Keep seed warm and shaded from light until it germinates. Tall and productive, this strain is a florist favorite for filler and everlasting bunches. Harvest the pure white heads once firm and dry them by hanging in a dark airy spot.

planting.note: Sow indoors and move outdoors after the frost date for long-stemmed cuts.

directions: Press seed into warm mix and keep it dark until sprouts show. This tall Rio Grande type revels in heat and drought and blooms tirelessly. Bright orange bracts add unusual warm color to fresh and dried arrangements.

planting.note: Start indoors six to eight weeks early and transplant after frost.

directions: Sow warm and keep the tray dark until germination, then grow in full light. This vigorous strain carries large bright red heads on strong upright stems. Cut when the color is full for lasting fresh bouquets or hang to dry as everlastings.

planting.note: Raise indoors and plant out after frost for garden beds or containers.

directions: Barely cover seed and keep it warm and dark for even sprouting. This mid-height series branches well and stays covered in bloom without deadheading. Rich purple heads shine in beds, pots, and pollinator plantings all summer.

planting.note: Sow indoors early and transplant into warm soil after frost.

directions: Keep seed warm and shaded until germination, then grow on brightly. Well-branched and free-flowering, it performs in beds and mixed containers alike. Clear pink heads attract butterflies and hold up in summer heat.

planting.note: Start indoors and set out once the danger of frost is past.

directions: Sow shallowly in warmth and keep dark until sprouts appear. This tidy series holds its shape in beds and pots through the hottest weather. Crisp white heads bloom continuously and dry cleanly for arrangements.

planting.note: Grow transplants indoors and plant out after frost for beds or cutting.

directions: Press seed into warm mix and keep it dark until it germinates. This uniform garden and cut-flower series branches freely on strong stems. Deep purple-red heads bloom heavily from summer to frost and dry beautifully.

planting.note: Sow indoors early and transplant after the last frost.

directions: Keep seed warm and dark until sprouting, then grow with good light. Well-branched and vigorous, this selection suits both borders and cutting rows. Pure white heads cut fresh or dry well as everlastings.

planting.note: Start seed indoors and set plants out into warm soil.

directions: Sow warm and keep dark for even germination, then grow on in full sun. This series carries two-toned rose and white heads on branching stems. Bloom is heavy and long, and the heads keep their bicolor pattern when dried.

planting.note: Raise indoors and plant out after frost for beds or bouquets.

directions: Barely cover seed and keep it warm and dark until sprouts show. This vigorous branching series holds up in heat and needs no deadheading. Soft pink heads bloom all summer and are excellent fresh or dried.

planting.note: Start indoors early and give this large variety room after transplanting past frost.

directions: Sow warm and dark, then space the vigorous seedlings well apart. This award-winning giant forms a big airy plant topped with hundreds of hot-pink heads tipped in gold. It blooms until hard frost, laughs at heat and humidity, and draws butterflies all day.

planting.note: Usually sold as vegetative plants; set out after all frost has passed.

directions: Plant nursery starts after the soil warms, spacing them for good airflow. This mounding selection throws up a haze of bright pink heads on wiry stems well above the foliage. It flowers nonstop through heat with no deadheading and is a magnet for butterflies.

planting.note: Sow indoors early and transplant after frost for cutting rows.

directions: Keep seed warm and dark until it germinates, then grow on with strong light. Bred for cutting, this series produces large round heads on long uniform stems. Harvest when heads are firm for fresh bunches or dry them for everlastings.

planting.note: Start indoors and plant out after the last frost.

directions: Barely cover seed and provide bottom heat and darkness for germination. This cut-flower series gives big clean heads on tall straight stems. Cut for vases or hang to dry once the heads have fully colored.

planting.note: Raise indoors early and transplant into warm soil for cut stems.

directions: Sow warm and dark, then grow with plenty of light for sturdy stems. This uniform cutting strain blooms heavily on long, straight stalks. Soft lavender heads are prized for both fresh and dried work.

planting.note: Start indoors and plant out after frost for large-headed cut flowers.

directions: Keep seed warm and shaded until germination, then grow on brightly. This cut-flower series is selected for oversized heads on long stems. Harvest when heads are firm and full for premium fresh or dried bunches.

planting.note: Sow indoors early and transplant after the danger of frost passes.

directions: Press seed into warm mix and keep it dark until sprouts appear. This selection carries distinctive rose-and-white two-toned heads on branching plants. Bloom continues all summer and the bicolor pattern persists when dried.

planting.note: Sow outdoors once the soil has warmed and frost danger has passed.

directions: Push seeds about an inch deep directly into warm garden soil and keep the bed evenly moist until sprouts appear. Stake the tall single stalks in windy spots so the heavy heads do not topple. The huge nodding disks fill with edible striped seeds by late summer; cut heads when the backs turn brown and dry them for snacking or bird feed.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last spring frost when soil is warm.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in full sun and thin the seedlings to stand about a foot and a half apart. This branching type throws many side blooms in shades of gold, bronze, and mahogany over a long stretch. Snip stems for the vase just as the petals lift away from the center disk.

planting.note: Sow in warm soil after frost; suits containers and small beds.

directions: Set seeds an inch deep in a sunny pot or border and water regularly while young. The compact plants carry fluffy, fully double golden pompons that look almost furry. Pinch spent blooms to keep fresh flowers coming through the season.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil once frost has passed.

directions: Sow an inch deep in full sun and thin so plants have room to branch. Deep crimson petals surround a near-black disk, and the multi-stemmed habit gives many cutting stems. Harvest for arrangements as the outer petals just begin to unfurl.

planting.note: Sow directly after frost; a favorite for pollinator plantings.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in a sunny, open spot with room to spread. The tall, well-branched plants carry dozens of soft pale-yellow blooms that bees adore. Leave some heads to ripen for finches, or cut stems for long-lasting bouquets.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; pollenless so cleaner for cutting.

directions: Sow an inch deep in full sun and keep young plants watered as they establish. The deep mahogany-red blooms are pollenless, so they drop no messy powder on tabletops. Cut stems in the cool of the morning as petals begin to open.

planting.note: Sow into warm soil after the last frost.

directions: Set seeds an inch deep in full sun and thin to give the branching plants space. Small creamy-white blooms with dark centers appear in airy sprays rather than one big head. Cut freely for delicate arrangements, and the plant will keep producing side flowers.

planting.note: Sow after frost; short enough for pots and front borders.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in a sunny container or bed edge and water steadily. Despite the short stature the golden heads grow surprisingly large, sometimes ten inches across. Leave the ripe seed heads on the plant to feed birds in early fall.

planting.note: Direct sow in succession for a steady cut-flower supply.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep and spaced closely to force straight single stems for cutting. This pollenless florist type produces one clean orange bloom per plant on a strong stalk. Cut when the petals first reflex, and sow a fresh batch every week or two for continuous harvest.

planting.note: Sow in blocks or succession for reliable cut stems.

directions: Plant an inch deep at close spacing to grow uniform single-stem blooms. The golden-yellow flowers are pollenless and hold well in the vase, making this a florist standard. Harvest as the bloom just opens and repeat sowings for weeks of flowers.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; an award-winning bicolor.

directions: Sow an inch deep in full sun and thin to give the branching plants elbow room. Each bloom rings a dark center with red at the petal base fading to gold at the tips. The pollenless flowers are excellent for cutting and beloved by pollinators.

planting.note: Sow after frost; a deep red, pollenless branching type.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in full sun and thin as they grow. The blooms open wine-red with subtle bronze highlights and shed no pollen. Many side stems follow the first flower, so cut often to encourage more.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; soft creamy blooms for cutting.

directions: Sow an inch deep in a sunny bed and thin the seedlings to space the branching plants. Pale lemon-cream petals surround a dark chocolate center on many slender stems. Snip blooms as they open for a soft, elegant bouquet.

planting.note: Sow after frost; a sturdy award-winning branching type.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in full sun and thin to stand well apart. Rich tangerine-orange petals ring a dark disk on strong, wind-resistant stems. The plant keeps throwing new blooms after cutting, so harvest generously.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil; a warm-toned branching mix.

directions: Sow an inch deep in full sun and thin so the tall plants can branch freely. Blooms range through deep red, rust, and bronze with a sunset glow. Cut stems as petals lift, and enjoy the mix of shades in one bouquet.

planting.note: Sow after frost; grows very tall with large heads.

directions: Push seeds an inch deep into warm, well-drained soil in a sheltered sunny spot. These towering stalks can top twelve feet and carry heads a foot wide, so give them steady water and a stake. Let the head dry on the plant to harvest the plump edible seeds.

planting.note: Direct sow in warm soil for a tall seed-producing plant.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep once the soil warms and keep the bed moist during establishment. The single massive head fills with striped seeds prized for roasting and for wildlife. Cut the head when the back yellows then browns, and cure it in a dry, airy place.

planting.note: Sow after frost; very compact, ideal for pots.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in a sunny pot or the front of a bed and water consistently. This tidy dwarf makes a single classic yellow bloom with a dark center on a short, strong stem. It flowers fast from seed, so sow in succession for a long show.

planting.note: Sow after frost; an award-winning dwarf for containers.

directions: Sow an inch deep in full sun and water often since the compact plants dry out quickly in pots. Each little plant carries many bright yellow, dark-centered blooms over a long window. Remove faded flowers to keep the display fresh.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; among the tallest sunflowers.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in a sheltered sunny spot and stake early to guard against wind. The stalks soar well past ten feet and hold large golden heads aloft. Water deeply in dry spells so the great height is well supported.

planting.note: Sow after frost; prized for enormous seed heads.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in rich soil and feed the plants to fuel their size. The single heads can reach two feet across and brim with large seeds. Support the stalk as the head fills, and dry the head to collect the harvest.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; a tall, fully double form.

directions: Sow an inch deep in full sun and thin so the plants stand well apart. Big fluffy golden pompons with little visible center top the tall stems. Cut a few for dramatic bouquets and leave the rest for a bold garden display.

planting.note: Sow after frost; a short branching color mix.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in full sun and thin to a foot or so apart. The compact branching plants bear many blooms in cream, gold, red, and bronze. Cut often to enjoy the color range and keep new flowers forming.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; a compact bicolor for small spaces.

directions: Sow an inch deep in a sunny bed or large pot and keep the soil evenly moist. The short branching plants produce many red-and-gold bicolor blooms with dark centers. Deadhead spent flowers to prolong the bright display.

planting.note: Sow after frost; a soft rose-and-gold bicolor.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in full sun and thin as the seedlings grow. Petals blend rosy pink at the base into pale lemon tips around a dark center. The pollenless branching plants give many clean stems for cutting.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; warm earthy tones on tall plants.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in full sun and thin the tall branching plants for airflow. Blooms glow in rich shades of rust, bronze, brick, and deep gold. Cut stems as the petals open for a moody, autumn-toned arrangement.

planting.note: Sow after frost; a well-branched pollenless cut type.

directions: Sow an inch deep in full sun and thin to give the branching plants room. Golden-yellow, pollenless blooms appear on many uniform stems over a long season. Cut regularly to spur the next flush of flowers.

planting.note: Set out transplants after frost; a long-blooming branching type.

directions: Plant nursery starts at their original depth once nights stay warm and water them in well. This heavily branching selection covers itself in small golden blooms for months rather than a single flush. Feed occasionally and remove old flowers to keep the bushy plant blooming.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; a striking mahogany-and-gold bicolor.

directions: Sow an inch deep in full sun and thin to space the branching plants. Each bloom is banded deep mahogany-red at the center ringed by bright gold tips. Cut freely for arrangements as the plant keeps forming side blooms.

planting.note: Sow after frost; a multi-colored branching blend.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in full sun and thin the seedlings to stand apart. The tall branching plants bloom in a painterly mix of yellow, gold, bronze, and rust. Cut stems as petals open to enjoy the varied palette indoors.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; soft creamy-yellow blooms.

directions: Sow an inch deep in full sun and thin as the branching plants develop. The pale buttery-cream petals surround a soft chocolate center for an elegant look. Pollenless stems stay clean in the vase, so cut generously.

planting.note: Sow after frost; a compact, award-winning dusky-rose dwarf.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in a sunny bed or pot and keep young plants watered. The compact stems carry rosy purple-red petals tipped with lighter edges around a dark eye. Reddish foliage adds interest, and cutting spent blooms keeps new ones coming.

planting.note: Direct sow in succession; a fast double single-stem cut type.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep at close spacing to grow straight single stems. Each plant makes one fluffy double orange bloom that is pollenless and vase-friendly. Because it flowers quickly, sow every week or two for an ongoing cut supply.

planting.note: Sow after frost; a pollenless dark-red florist type.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in full sun and thin to encourage upright stems. Deep red-mahogany blooms shed no pollen, keeping them tidy for cutting. Harvest as the petals reflex and expect several side blooms after the first.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; a rich burgundy cut-flower hybrid.

directions: Sow an inch deep in full sun and thin as the plants grow. The velvety wine-red blooms hold their deep color well and are pollenless. Cut stems in early morning as the petals begin to open for the longest vase life.

planting.note: Sow in succession; a classic golden florist single-stem.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep and closely spaced to grow uniform straight stems. The golden-yellow, dark-centered, pollenless blooms are a florist favorite. Harvest as the flower opens and sow again for a steady cut-flower run.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; a smaller-flowered orange cut type.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in full sun and thin to space the branching plants. Rich deep-orange blooms are held on slender stems at a handy size for bouquets. The pollenless flowers cut cleanly, and plants rebloom after harvest.

planting.note: Sow after frost; a compact fiery bicolor.

directions: Sow an inch deep in full sun and thin the seedlings to give room. Blooms open in bands of red and orange toward gold tips around a dark center. The branching habit gives many stems, so cut often for warm-toned bouquets.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; a soft golden branching type.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in full sun and thin as the plants branch. Warm golden blooms with pointed petals and a green-gold center recall the painter's studies. Cut freely for arrangements and the plants keep forming new flowers.

planting.note: Sow in succession; a pale single-stem florist type.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep at tight spacing to force clean single stems. The creamy near-white petals ring a dark center on a pollenless bloom. Cut as the flower opens and stagger sowings for weeks of pale blooms.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; a very compact branching bicolor.

directions: Sow an inch deep in full sun and water the compact plants regularly. Small red-and-yellow bicolor blooms cover the dwarf, well-branched plants for weeks. It is pollenless and tidy, so it works well in pots and along path edges.

planting.note: Sow after frost; a compact fully double golden form.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in a sunny bed or roomy container and keep watered. The short plants carry rounded, fully double gold pompons with little visible center. Deadhead faded flowers to keep the plush blooms coming.

planting.note: Raise transplants indoors a month or so ahead of the last frost for the earliest large double blooms.

directions: Surface-sow and press seed into warm, moist mix, leaving it uncovered or barely dusted. Space the tall stems so air moves freely and stake in windy sites, as the fully double heads can grow heavy. Harvest for everlasting bouquets when a couple of outer bract rows have lifted; blooms hold color for months once air-dried.

planting.note: Best started indoors for cut-flower use so the long, sturdy stems have a full season to develop.

directions: Sow onto the surface of a warm seed mix and keep in bright light until sprouts appear in one to two weeks. Grow in full sun with sparing water and avoid rich feeding, which softens the stems. Cut the extra-large flowers for drying when the outer bracts just begin to reflex, leaving the button center still tight.

planting.note: Compact enough for beds, edging and containers; start indoors or direct-sow after frost.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep it warm and evenly moist until germination. This dwarf strain needs full sun and free-draining soil, staying tidy without staking. Enjoy the mix of jewel-toned blooms in the garden or snip them young for small dried arrangements.

planting.note: Tall, big-headed strain for the back of a border or a dedicated cutting row.

directions: Press the fine seed onto warm mix and leave it exposed to light while it sprouts. Give it full sun, lean soil and room to branch, staking tall stems where wind is strong. Cut for everlasting bunches as the first bract rows open, then dry upside down out of direct sun.

planting.note: A warm coppery-red selection prized for autumn-toned dried work.

directions: Surface-sow and keep the seed warm, moist and lightly lit until it germinates. Plant out in full sun after frost and water sparingly once established. The rich rusty bracts hold their color exceptionally well, so cut them for drying just as they start to open.

planting.note: An intense fiery scarlet-orange selection for hot color in beds and bouquets.

directions: Scatter seed on the surface of a warm mix and press gently, leaving it uncovered for light. Grow in full sun with restrained watering to keep stems strong. Pick the blazing bracts for fresh or dried use as the outer rows begin to open and before the center loosens.

planting.note: Soft pearly rose-pink shade favored for pastel dried arrangements.

directions: Barely cover the seed and hold it warm and lightly moist until sprouts show. Set plants in full sun and thin, well-drained ground, watering only when dry. Cut the shimmering pink heads for drying at the first hint of open bracts to keep the tidy button shape.

planting.note: A deep wine to purplish-red shade that adds darker tones to a mixed cutting patch.

directions: Surface-sow, press in and keep warm and bright while the seed germinates. Grow in full sun with lean soil and modest water for the sturdiest stems. Harvest the deep-toned bracts for everlastings as they begin to open, then dry them out of strong light to preserve depth of color.

planting.note: Clean white selection that blends with any color scheme in dried work.

directions: Press the tiny seed onto warm, moist mix and leave it uncovered for light. Give plants full sun and sharp drainage, watering sparingly through summer. Cut the ivory heads early, before the centers loosen, since white bracts discolor quickly if picked too open.

planting.note: A soft pale primrose-yellow shade with a frosted look, gentle among brighter tones.

directions: Surface-sow onto a warm mix and keep it lightly moist and bright until germination. Plant in full sun and free-draining soil, holding back on fertilizer and water. Snip the pale sulphur heads for drying at the first sign of opening bracts for the cleanest color.

planting.note: Warm salmon-pink shade popular in cottage-style cutting gardens.

directions: Barely cover the fine seed and keep it warm and evenly moist until it sprouts. Grow in full sun with lean, well-drained soil and sparing water. Cut the salmon bracts for fresh or dried bouquets as the outer rows lift and the center stays closed.

planting.note: A blend of soft rose, apricot, cream and pale yellow tones for gentle bouquets.

directions: Surface-sow onto warm mix, press lightly and keep bright until germination. Site in full sun with restrained watering to firm the stems. Cut a range of gentle shades for drying just as the first bracts unfold, then hang to preserve.

planting.note: A fiery scarlet dwarf for edging, pots and window boxes.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep it warm and lightly moist until it germinates. This compact plant thrives in full sun and needs no staking, holding tidy in containers. Deadhead spent heads or cut a few bright red blooms young for miniature dried bunches.

planting.note: Sold as vegetatively propagated plants; set out after all danger of frost.

directions: Plant nursery starts at the same depth they grew in the pot once nights stay mild. Give full sun, light soil and moderate water, since this vegetative series branches freely without pinching. Deep bronze-red daisy-like flowers keep coming all summer, so shear lightly if bloom slows.

planting.note: Vegetative plants best set out after the last frost into warm soil.

directions: Set container-grown starts out level with the surrounding soil once the weather warms. Grow in full sun with sparing water for the best flowering and mounded habit. The orange-red bicolor blooms are excellent for pots and edging and continue right up to frost.

planting.note: Propagated from cuttings and planted out once frost danger has passed.

directions: Plant purchased starts at their original soil level after nights turn mild. Provide full sun and lean, free-draining soil, watering only when the surface dries. Warm golden-yellow flowers cover the compact mound through the season with little maintenance beyond a midsummer trim.

planting.note: Vegetative bedding selection set out after frost for containers and landscapes.

directions: Transplant nursery-grown plants into warm soil at the depth they were growing. Full sun and restrained watering keep the mound compact and free-flowering. The bright fiery-red heads are heat and drought tolerant and bloom continuously into autumn.

planting.note: Large-flowered vegetative type; plant out after frost into warm ground.

directions: Set purchased plants out at their original depth once frost has passed. Give full sun and light, well-drained soil, feeding lightly and watering with restraint. This vigorous selection carries unusually large golden blooms all summer and rebounds well after a midseason shearing.

planting.note: Large-flowered vegetative type; plant out after the last frost.

directions: Plant nursery starts level with the surrounding soil after nights stay warm. Full sun, sharp drainage and modest water bring out the strongest flowering. Big glowing-orange heads appear from early summer to frost and make excellent long-lasting cut flowers.

subcategory: annual foliage (tender perennial grown as annual)

planting.note: Grown mainly for trailing silver foliage; plant out from cuttings after frost.

directions: Plant a rooted cutting or nursery start after frost, where the woolly silver stems can trail over pot edges or spill through beds. Give full sun and dry-leaning soil, since soggy conditions cause rot on this drought-tolerant plant. Trim stems any time to shape it; the small cream flowers are secondary to the felted foliage.

subcategory: annual foliage (tender perennial grown as annual)

planting.note: Chartreuse-leaved trailing form for containers and hanging baskets.

directions: Set out rooted cuttings after frost where the soft lime-green foliage can cascade. Give bright light with some afternoon shade in hot regions to keep the color fresh, and avoid overwatering. Pinch tips to keep growth full and use the trailing stems to soften the edges of mixed plantings.

subcategory: annual foliage (tender perennial grown as annual)

planting.note: Silver-and-cream variegated trailer for baskets and combination pots.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings after frost in full to partial sun with free-draining soil. Keep it on the dry side, as the felted variegated leaves resist drought but dislike wet roots. Shear back leggy stems through summer to maintain a dense, trailing habit in containers.

subcategory: annual foliage (tender perennial grown as annual)

planting.note: Grown for fine silver needle-like foliage in beds and containers.

directions: Plant nursery starts after frost in full sun and light, well-drained soil. The slender silver foliage is the main feature, so shear occasionally to keep the mound dense and upright. Tolerant of heat and drought, it pairs well with brighter bedding and needs little water once settled.

planting.note: Hardy dry-meadow everlasting; start indoors early or sow outdoors in spring.

directions: Surface-sow onto a well-drained mix and leave the seed uncovered, since light aids germination. Grow in full sun and sandy, poor soil that stays on the dry side. Small clusters of golden-yellow papery flowers appear in summer and dry beautifully; cut them just as they open for lasting arrangements.

planting.note: Aromatic silver-leaved subshrub of dry Mediterranean slopes.

directions: Sow onto the surface of a gritty mix and barely cover, expecting slow germination. Plant in full sun and very sharply drained soil, keeping it dry once rooted. Rounded clusters of bright yellow papery blooms open in summer above the fragrant silver foliage and dry well.

planting.note: Grown as an annual for its rich red-brown papery bracts.

directions: Surface-sow onto warm mix and press lightly, keeping the seed in bright light until it sprouts. Grow in full sun and dry, well-drained soil with sparing water. The small deep red everlasting flowers hold their color when dried, so cut stems as the bracts open for craft and floral work.
